steps.heading: What is Artificial Intelligence?
steps.body: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the ability of a computer system to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence — like understanding language, recognizing patterns, making decisions, and solving problems.

steps.heading: AI is everywhere
steps.body: AI powers the recommendations on Netflix, spam filters in your email, voice assistants like Siri and Alexa, and the autocomplete on your keyboard. It's not science fiction — it's part of everyday life.

steps.question: Which of these is an example of AI?
options: A thermostat turning on the heat
A system that recommends movies based on your history
A clock showing the current time

steps.heading: Narrow AI: Built for one thing
steps.body: Almost all AI today is Narrow AI — systems designed to excel at a single task. Chess engines, image classifiers, and spam detectors are all narrow AI. They are superhuman at their specific task but useless outside it.

steps.heading: General AI: Still a goal
steps.body: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) would match or exceed human intelligence across any task. It doesn't exist yet — despite what headlines suggest. Today's LLMs are impressive but remain narrow tools, not general reasoners.

steps.question: Which best describes today's AI systems like ChatGPT?
options: Narrow AI with broad language capabilities

steps.heading: AI is not magic
steps.body: AI is pattern recognition at scale. It doesn't understand the world the way humans do — it finds statistical relationships in data. When an AI writes a poem, it's not feeling creative; it's generating text that resembles poetry it was trained on.

steps.heading: Common myths debunked
steps.body: Myth: AI is always right. Fact: AI confidently makes mistakes. Myth: AI thinks like humans. Fact: AI processes tokens, not concepts. Myth: AI will replace all jobs. Fact: AI augments work — it changes how work gets done, not whether it gets done.

steps.question: Why do AI systems sometimes produce confident but wrong answers?
options: They generate statistically likely text, not verified facts
They don't have enough processing power

title: What is a Large Language Model?

steps.heading: What is a Large Language Model?
steps.body: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a type of AI trained on massive amounts of text. It learns to predict what words come next in a sentence — and through that process develops a deep understanding of language, facts, and reasoning.

steps.heading: How does it generate text?
steps.body: When you send a message to an LLM like ChatGPT, it reads your input and generates a response one token at a time — always choosing the most likely next token based on everything it has learned during training.

steps.question: What does an LLM primarily learn to do during training?
options: Predict the next word in a sequence
Search the internet in real time

steps.heading: What is a token?
steps.body: LLMs don't process words — they process tokens. A token is roughly 3-4 characters or about 0.75 words. The word 'unhappy' might be one token, while 'unbelievable' could be split into 'un', 'believ', 'able'. Tokenization is how models break text into manageable pieces.

steps.heading: Why tokens matter
steps.body: Every LLM has a context window — the maximum number of tokens it can process at once. GPT-4 supports ~128,000 tokens. Longer conversations cost more tokens and can hit the limit. Understanding tokens helps you write efficient prompts and know when context is getting cut off.

steps.question: Approximately how many words is 1,000 tokens?

steps.heading: Training: building the model
steps.body: Training is the expensive, one-time process where an LLM learns from billions of text examples. The model adjusts its internal parameters to get better at predicting the next token. This requires massive compute and takes weeks or months.

steps.heading: Inference: using the model
steps.body: Inference is what happens every time you send a message to ChatGPT. The trained model uses its fixed parameters to generate a response. Inference is much cheaper than training but still requires significant compute — which is why API calls cost money.

steps.question: What happens during 'inference' in an LLM?
options: The model updates its parameters from new data
The model uses its trained parameters to generate a response
The model searches a database for answers
The model is retrained on your conversation

steps.heading: Under the hood
steps.body: ChatGPT is built on GPT-4, a large language model from OpenAI. When you send a message, it's encoded into tokens, passed through the model's neural network layers, and the output tokens are decoded back into text — all in seconds.

steps.heading: RLHF: Making it helpful
steps.body: Raw LLMs predict text without caring if it's helpful or safe. ChatGPT uses Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) — human raters score outputs, and the model is fine-tuned to produce responses humans prefer. This is why it feels like a conversation, not a text predictor.

steps.question: What technique makes ChatGPT feel conversational and helpful?
options: It has access to the internet
It stores your previous conversations forever
It uses a different AI for each response

steps.heading: What is a context window?
steps.body: A context window is everything the model can 'see' at once — your entire conversation history, the system prompt, and your current message. Think of it as the model's working memory. When the window fills up, older messages get dropped.

steps.heading: Practical implications
steps.body: In a long conversation, ChatGPT may 'forget' what you said at the start. To work around this, summarize earlier context, start a new thread for a new topic, or explicitly re-state important facts. Context management is a key prompt engineering skill.

steps.question: What happens when a conversation exceeds the context window limit?
options: The model starts ignoring all new messages
The oldest messages are dropped from the context
The model automatically summarizes the conversation

steps.heading: The three message types
steps.body: Most LLM APIs use three message roles: System, User, and Assistant. The System message sets the model's behavior and persona before the conversation starts. User messages are what you send. Assistant messages are the model's responses.

steps.heading: System prompts are powerful
steps.body: The system prompt is your opportunity to define how the model behaves throughout the conversation. You can set its persona ('You are a senior engineer'), restrict its scope ('Only answer coding questions'), or define its output format ('Always respond in JSON'). Great products are built on great system prompts.

steps.question: Where would you define 'You are a helpful customer support agent' in the OpenAI API?

steps.heading: AI's genuine strengths
steps.body: Modern AI excels at: summarizing and transforming text, drafting content, writing and explaining code, translating languages, answering questions from provided context, brainstorming ideas, and pattern recognition in large datasets. These are tasks where speed and breadth matter more than guaranteed accuracy.

steps.heading: The power of scale
steps.body: What makes AI powerful is not that it does things humans can't — it's that it does things instantly at massive scale. A task that takes a human an hour (summarizing a report, translating a document) takes an LLM seconds. This changes how we allocate our time and attention.

steps.question: Which task is AI best suited for?
options: Providing legal advice with guaranteed accuracy

steps.heading: The real limitations
steps.body: AI has well-known failure modes: it hallucinate facts with false confidence, it has a training knowledge cutoff, it cannot access real-time information without tools, it has no memory across sessions by default, and it cannot take actions in the world unless given specific tools.

steps.heading: Accuracy vs confidence
steps.body: The most dangerous AI behavior is confident wrongness. An LLM will state false information in the same tone as true information. Never use AI as a sole source for facts that matter — always verify important claims, especially in legal, medical, financial, or technical contexts.

steps.question: Why is AI's 'hallucination' problem particularly dangerous?
options: It causes the model to refuse questions
False information is presented with the same confidence as true information
It only happens with math questions

steps.heading: Why AI safety matters
steps.body: AI safety covers alignment (making AI do what we intend), robustness (making AI behave well in unexpected situations), and oversight (keeping humans in control). As AI systems become more capable and are given more autonomy, these concerns become increasingly important.

steps.heading: Practical safety today
steps.body: Right now, AI safety is mostly about responsible use: don't over-trust AI outputs, maintain human review for high-stakes decisions, be aware of bias in training data, and understand that AI systems can be manipulated via prompt injection. Responsible AI use is everyone's job.

steps.question: What is 'AI alignment'?
options: Making sure AI text is formatted correctly
Ensuring AI systems behave according to human intentions and values
A feature that improves AI response speed

steps.heading: OpenAI and the GPT family
steps.body: OpenAI built the GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) series. ChatGPT, launched in November 2022, became the fastest-growing consumer app in history. The underlying model (GPT-4) is also available via API, powering thousands of AI products and tools.

steps.heading: Strengths and use cases
steps.body: ChatGPT excels at general-purpose tasks: writing, coding assistance, data analysis, and image understanding (with GPT-4V). It integrates with plugins and tools, making it a versatile platform. The free tier uses GPT-3.5; paid tiers access GPT-4.

steps.question: What model powers the paid version of ChatGPT?

steps.heading: Constitutional AI
steps.body: Claude is Anthropic's AI assistant, built with a focus on safety and helpfulness. Anthropic developed 'Constitutional AI' — a technique where the model is trained to critique and revise its own outputs against a set of principles. This makes Claude less likely to produce harmful content.

steps.heading: Claude's strengths
steps.body: Claude has a large context window (200K tokens), making it excellent for analyzing long documents. It tends to be careful with nuance, good at following complex instructions, and excels at tasks requiring structured reasoning. Claude is a strong choice for technical writing, code review, and document analysis.

steps.question: What makes Claude's training approach distinct?
options: It was trained only on code
It uses Constitutional AI to guide safety
It was built by the same team as ChatGPT

steps.heading: The open-source movement
steps.body: Not all powerful AI is closed and proprietary. Meta's LLaMA family, Mistral, and Phi (from Microsoft) are open-weight models that anyone can download and run. This enables private deployments, fine-tuning for specific domains, and AI without sending data to third-party servers.

steps.heading: Trade-offs
steps.body: Open models are generally less capable than frontier proprietary models, but the gap is closing fast. Tools like Ollama let you run LLaMA locally on a laptop. For many use cases — particularly those with privacy requirements or specific domain expertise — open-source models are the right choice.

steps.question: What is a key advantage of open-weight AI models over proprietary ones?
options: They can be run locally without sending data to third parties

title: The Goal: What Do You Want?

steps.heading: Start with the output
steps.body: Every great prompt starts with a clear goal. Before writing anything, ask: what exactly do I want the model to produce? A summary? A list? A piece of code? An email? Being vague about your goal produces vague results. Specificity is the foundation of prompt engineering.

steps.heading: Examples of clear goals
steps.body: Weak: 'Help me with my email.' Strong: 'Write a 3-sentence follow-up email to a client who hasn't responded in 5 days, professional but friendly in tone.' The strong version specifies the type, length, audience, and tone — leaving nothing for the model to guess.

steps.question: Which prompt has the clearest goal?
options: Write a Python function that takes a list of integers and returns the top 3 largest values

steps.heading: Why context matters
steps.body: Context tells the model who you are, what situation you're in, and what background is relevant. Without it, the model makes assumptions — and those assumptions may be completely wrong for your use case. More relevant context = better outputs.

steps.heading: Types of context
steps.body: Context includes: your role ('I'm a senior DevOps engineer'), your audience ('explaining this to a non-technical manager'), the situation ('we're migrating a monolith to microservices'), and relevant constraints ('the system uses Python 3.8, no third-party libraries'). Each piece guides the model toward a more useful response.

steps.question: What does providing context in a prompt primarily achieve?
options: Guides the model to produce more relevant and tailored output
Prevents the model from making up information

steps.heading: Define what you don't want
steps.body: Constraints are as important as goals. They narrow the solution space and prevent wasted output. Common constraints: length ('in under 100 words'), format ('as a bulleted list'), style ('no jargon'), scope ('only cover X, not Y'), and audience ('suitable for a 10-year-old').

steps.heading: Specify the output format
steps.body: Telling the model what format to use eliminates guessing. 'Respond as a JSON object with keys: summary, action_items, priority' produces machine-parseable output. 'Write a numbered list' produces scannable content. Format instructions are one of the most effective ways to make AI output immediately useful.

steps.question: Why should you specify an output format in your prompt?
options: The model cannot produce output without a format instruction
It prevents the model from making up data
It ensures the output is immediately usable without reformatting

steps.heading: Clarity beats cleverness
steps.body: The best prompts are straightforward. Don't try to be indirect or 'trick' the model — just say what you want. Use active voice, imperative sentences, and specific verbs. 'List the top 5 security risks for a Node.js app' is better than 'Could you perhaps tell me something about security in Node.js applications?'

steps.heading: Precision in language
steps.body: Words like 'improve', 'fix', 'make better' are ambiguous. Replace them with precise instructions: 'Fix the type errors', 'Refactor to reduce duplication', 'Improve readability by extracting this logic into a named function'. The more specific your verbs and nouns, the better the output.

steps.question: Which prompt uses the clearest and most direct language?
options: Maybe you could improve this somehow
Refactor this function to use early returns instead of nested if statements
I think there might be a way to clean this up

steps.heading: Chain of thought
steps.body: For complex problems, tell the model to think step by step before giving an answer. Phrases like 'Think through this carefully before responding' or 'Let's work through this step by step' activate chain-of-thought reasoning — the model externalizes its reasoning, which significantly improves accuracy on hard problems.

steps.heading: Breaking down tasks
steps.body: Complex tasks should be broken into smaller prompts rather than one massive request. Instead of 'Build me a full authentication system', try: first 'Design the data model for user authentication', then 'Write the registration endpoint', then 'Write the login endpoint'. Each focused prompt produces better output.

steps.question: Why does asking an LLM to 'think step by step' improve accuracy?
options: It makes the model slower and more careful
It externalizes the reasoning process, reducing logical errors
It forces the model to search the internet

steps.heading: The model knows nothing about you
steps.body: Every new conversation starts from zero. The model doesn't know your tech stack, your team's conventions, your past decisions, or your constraints. Whatever context you don't provide, the model fills in from its training data — which may be completely wrong for your situation.

steps.heading: Context as a force multiplier
steps.body: Adding the right context to a prompt can be the difference between a useless answer and a perfect one. 'Review this code' produces generic advice. 'Review this code — it runs on AWS Lambda, must be under 200ms cold start, and uses our company's custom error handling pattern' produces actionable, specific feedback.

steps.question: What does the LLM use to fill in information you don't provide in a prompt?
options: Statistical patterns from its training data

steps.heading: Few-shot prompting
steps.body: Few-shot prompting means showing the model 1-3 examples of the input-output pattern you want before asking it to process your actual input. Instead of describing the format, you demonstrate it. This is one of the most reliable techniques for getting consistent, formatted output.

steps.heading: When to use examples
steps.body: Use examples when: the output format is unusual, the task is ambiguous, you need a specific tone or style, or you're transforming data. A single example of input → output is often worth a paragraph of description. The model pattern-matches from your example and applies it reliably.

steps.question: What is 'few-shot prompting'?
options: Providing examples of the desired input-output pattern in the prompt
Using the model for only a few tasks

steps.heading: JSON output from LLMs
steps.body: Modern LLMs can produce valid JSON reliably when asked. This is powerful — it bridges natural language and code. You can prompt: 'Extract the key information from this text and return a JSON object with fields: name, date, location, summary.' The output is immediately usable by code.

steps.heading: Defining the schema
steps.body: The more precisely you define the JSON schema, the better. Specify field names, types, and what each should contain. 'Return a JSON object with: { "severity": "low|medium|high", "category": string, "description": string, "remediation": string }' leaves no room for inconsistency.

steps.question: When is requesting JSON output from an LLM most useful?
options: When you want more creative responses
When the output needs to be processed programmatically
When you want the model to think harder

steps.heading: Formatting as a feature
steps.body: LLMs support Markdown formatting. Asking for a table, numbered list, or headers significantly affects how the output is structured and consumed. A comparison prompt like 'Compare X and Y' produces better output when you add 'present as a Markdown table with columns: Feature, X, Y'.

steps.heading: Match format to use case
steps.body: Use bullet points for quick-scan lists. Use numbered lists for steps or priority-ordered items. Use tables for comparisons or structured data. Use headers for long-form output you'll need to navigate. Use plain prose for human-readable narrative. Choosing the right format doubles the usefulness of the output.

steps.question: Which format is best for presenting a step-by-step setup process?

steps.heading: Setting a role
steps.body: Assigning a role to the model ('You are a senior security engineer', 'Act as a Socratic tutor') shifts its response style, vocabulary, and reasoning focus. Roles activate different 'modes' of the model's knowledge, making responses more targeted and expert in the assigned domain.

steps.heading: Persona prompting
steps.body: A persona goes beyond role — it defines personality, communication style, and constraints. 'You are Alex, a no-nonsense staff engineer who gives brutally honest code reviews. You prioritize correctness, maintainability, and performance in that order. You never sugarcoat feedback.' This creates consistent, predictable behavior across a long session.

steps.question: What is the primary benefit of role prompting?
options: It prevents the model from making errors
It activates relevant domain knowledge and adjusts response style
It gives the model access to real-time data

steps.heading: Chaining prompts
steps.body: Chain prompting means using the output of one prompt as the input to the next. Step 1: 'Analyze this bug report and identify the root cause.' Step 2: 'Given this root cause, suggest 3 possible fixes.' Step 3: 'Write the code for fix #2.' Chains break complex tasks into reliable stages.

steps.heading: Multi-step in a single prompt
steps.body: You can also instruct the model to perform multiple steps within one prompt. 'First, summarize the document in 3 bullet points. Second, identify any risks. Third, suggest next actions.' This is efficient but works best when steps are independent. If step 3 depends on complex reasoning from step 2, a separate prompt gives better results.

steps.question: What is the main advantage of chain prompting over a single large prompt?
options: It avoids the context window limit
Each step is focused, producing more reliable output at each stage
The model remembers previous chains automatically

steps.heading: What manual work costs you
steps.body: Manual work is anything a human does repeatedly that follows a predictable pattern: filling forms, running the same test suite, copying data between systems, clicking through deployment steps. Each repetition costs time, introduces human error, and scales poorly with team growth.

steps.heading: What automation gives you
steps.body: Automation replaces repetition with code. A script that takes 30 minutes to write can save 1 hour of manual work per week. Over a year that's 52 hours — more than a full work week. Automation also eliminates human error, produces consistent results, and frees engineers for higher-value work.

steps.question: Which scenario is most suitable for automation?
options: Running the same 50-step regression test every release

steps.heading: Types of automation
steps.body: Automation spans many domains: test automation (verifying software works), process automation (replacing manual business workflows), infrastructure automation (provisioning servers with code), and browser automation (controlling web browsers programmatically). Each has specialized tools, but the underlying principles are the same.

steps.heading: Triggers, actions, and assertions
steps.body: All automation follows a core pattern: Trigger (what starts the automation), Action (what it does), and Assertion (how it verifies success). A test triggers on code push, clicks a button, and asserts the result is visible. A workflow triggers on a calendar event, sends an email, and asserts delivery. This mental model applies everywhere.

steps.question: What are the three core components of any automation?

steps.heading: Calculating return on investment
steps.body: Before automating, calculate the ROI. How long does the manual task take? How often does it run? Multiply to get the weekly cost. If automation takes 4 hours to build and saves 2 hours/week, it pays for itself in 2 weeks. Everything after that is pure savings.

steps.heading: What not to automate
steps.body: Not everything is worth automating. One-off tasks, tasks that change frequently before they're stable, and tasks with complex human judgment are poor automation candidates. The XKCD 'Is It Worth the Time?' chart captures it perfectly: the more frequent and longer the task, the higher the ROI for automation.

steps.question: A manual task takes 10 minutes and runs daily. Building automation takes 8 hours. When does it break even?
options: Never — manual is always faster to start

steps.heading: Before you automate, map it
steps.body: The first step in automating a process is documenting it exactly as it currently works — every step, every decision point, every input and output. You cannot automate what you haven't clearly understood. Process mapping reveals hidden complexity, exceptions, and dependencies that break naive automation attempts.

steps.heading: Decision points are automation risk
steps.body: Decision points — places where a human chooses between paths based on judgment — are the hardest to automate. 'If the error looks weird, escalate' is not automatable without defining 'weird'. Map every decision point and decide: can this be rule-based? Does it need AI? Or does it need a human?

steps.question: Why is process mapping important before automation?
options: It makes the automation run faster
It reveals hidden complexity and decision points that affect the automation design
It is required by all automation frameworks

steps.heading: Defining inputs and outputs
steps.body: Every automation has inputs (what it receives) and outputs (what it produces). Clearly defining these is essential. Ambiguous inputs lead to fragile automation that breaks on edge cases. Undefined outputs make it impossible to verify success. Treat input/output contracts the same way you treat function signatures in code.

steps.heading: Managing dependencies
steps.body: Automation depends on external systems: databases, APIs, browsers, file systems. Each dependency is a failure point. Good automation handles dependency failures gracefully — it retries, falls back, or alerts a human. Ignoring dependencies is the #1 reason automation works in testing but fails in production.

steps.question: What is the primary risk of having unhandled dependencies in automation?
options: External failures silently break the automation with no alert

steps.heading: Browser automation built for the modern web
steps.body: Playwright is an open-source framework from Microsoft for automating modern web browsers — Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit. Unlike older tools like Selenium, Playwright was built with the modern web in mind: it handles async operations natively, has built-in waiting, and supports all modern browser features.

steps.heading: What you can do with Playwright
steps.body: With Playwright you can: write end-to-end tests that run in real browsers, scrape web data, automate repetitive browser tasks, capture screenshots and videos, test across multiple browsers with one API, and run tests in parallel at scale. It's the leading tool for browser automation in 2024.

steps.question: Which browsers does Playwright support?

steps.heading: Installation
steps.body: Install Playwright with: npm init playwright@latest. This sets up the project, installs browser binaries, and creates a sample test. The key files: playwright.config.ts (configuration), tests/ (your test files). Run tests with npx playwright test. View the HTML report with npx playwright show-report.

steps.heading: Project structure
steps.body: A typical Playwright project: tests/ for test files, fixtures/ for reusable setup, pages/ for Page Object Models, and playwright.config.ts at the root. The config controls browsers, base URL, timeouts, and parallelism. Start simple — one config, one tests/ folder — and expand as needed.

steps.question: What command runs all Playwright tests in a project?

steps.heading: Locators: the key to reliable tests
steps.body: A locator is how Playwright finds elements on the page. The best locators are user-facing: page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }) finds a button labeled 'Submit'. page.getByLabel('Email') finds the input for an email label. These are resilient to UI changes — they describe what the user sees, not implementation details.

steps.heading: Locator priority
steps.body: Use locators in this priority order: getByRole (most resilient), getByLabel, getByPlaceholder, getByText, getByTestId. Avoid CSS selectors like .btn-primary or XPath — these break when the UI is restyled. If a developer adds data-testid='submit-btn', use getByTestId. Never rely on position-based selectors like nth-child(3).

steps.question: Which is the most resilient way to locate a 'Login' button in Playwright?

steps.heading: Core actions
steps.body: Playwright actions mirror what a user does: click(), fill(), type(), check(), uncheck(), selectOption(), hover(), focus(), press(). Each action automatically waits for the element to be visible and interactive before proceeding — no manual waits or sleep() calls needed.

steps.heading: Navigation and waiting
steps.body: Navigate with await page.goto(url). Wait for a page load with await page.waitForLoadState('networkidle'). Wait for an element with await expect(locator).toBeVisible(). Playwright's auto-wait handles most cases, but you'll occasionally need explicit waits for complex async behavior.

steps.question: Why don't you typically need sleep() calls between Playwright actions?
options: Playwright runs too fast to need waits
Playwright automatically waits for elements to be ready before acting
You must disable async mode for waits to work
Sleep is not supported in Playwright

steps.heading: expect() is everything
steps.body: Assertions verify that the page state matches expectations. Use await expect(locator).toBeVisible() to assert an element is shown. Use await expect(page).toHaveURL('/dashboard') to assert navigation succeeded. Use await expect(locator).toHaveText('Welcome, David') to assert content. All expect() assertions automatically wait and retry.

steps.heading: Common assertion patterns
steps.body: Key assertions: toBeVisible/toBeHidden, toHaveText/toContainText, toHaveValue (for inputs), toBeChecked, toHaveURL, toHaveTitle, toHaveCount (for lists). Negative assertions use .not: await expect(locator).not.toBeVisible(). Assertions that fail show clear, readable error messages pointing to the failing expectation.

steps.question: What does await expect(locator).toBeVisible() do if the element is not yet visible?
options: Retries the assertion until it passes or the timeout expires

steps.heading: The Playwright Inspector
steps.body: Run PWDEBUG=1 npx playwright test to open the Playwright Inspector. It pauses execution and lets you step through your test action by action, see what the browser is doing at each step, and inspect element locators live. It's the fastest way to diagnose why a test is failing.

steps.heading: Trace Viewer
steps.body: The Trace Viewer records a full trace of a test run — every action, screenshot, network request, and console log — as a visual timeline you can replay. Enable traces in config: trace: 'on-first-retry'. After a test failure, run npx playwright show-report to open the trace. It shows you exactly what the browser saw at the moment of failure.

steps.question: What environment variable do you set to open the Playwright Inspector when running tests?

steps.heading: Naming your data
steps.body: A variable is a labeled container for storing a piece of information so your program can use it later. Think of it like a sticky note with a name on a box — you can look inside whenever you need to, and swap out what's inside without changing the label.

steps.heading: Declaring a variable
steps.body: In JavaScript, you create a variable with a keyword (like `let`) followed by a name and a value: `let score = 10;`. Now `score` holds the number 10, and you can use it anywhere in your code — print it, change it, or do math with it.

steps.question: What does a variable do in a program?
options: Stores a value so it can be used and reused later

steps.heading: Different kinds of values
steps.body: Every value in JavaScript has a type. The most common ones are strings (text, like "hello"), numbers (like 42 or 3.14), and booleans (true or false). Knowing a value's type tells you what you can do with it — you can do math with numbers, but not with text.

steps.heading: Spotting types in code
steps.body: Strings are wrapped in quotes: `"Apprentice"`. Numbers stand alone: `25`. Booleans are exactly `true` or `false`, with no quotes. Mixing them up — like writing `"25"` when you mean the number 25 — is one of the most common beginner mistakes.

steps.question: Which of these is a string in JavaScript?

steps.heading: Three ways to declare
steps.body: JavaScript gives you three keywords to create variables: `var` (the old way, rarely used now), `let` (for values that may change), and `const` (for values that should never be reassigned). Most modern code reaches for `const` first and only uses `let` when a value truly needs to change.

steps.heading: Why const by default?
steps.body: Using `const` signals to anyone reading your code — including future you — that this value is fixed, which makes programs easier to understand and debug. If you try to reassign a `const`, JavaScript throws an error, catching mistakes early.

steps.question: You're storing a user's name, which won't change after it's set. Which keyword is the best choice?

steps.heading: Reusable blocks of logic
steps.body: A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a task. Instead of writing the same steps over and over, you wrap them in a function once and "call" it by name whenever you need that task done — like a recipe you can follow again and again.

steps.heading: Defining and calling
steps.body: You define a function with the `function` keyword: `function greet() { console.log("Hello!"); }`. To run it, you call it by writing its name with parentheses: `greet();`. Defining a function doesn't run it — calling it does.

steps.question: What's the difference between defining a function and calling it?
options: Defining creates the function; calling it actually runs its code
Calling creates the function; defining runs it

steps.heading: Feeding data in
steps.body: Parameters are placeholders inside a function's parentheses that let you pass information in: `function greet(name) { console.log("Hi " + name); }`. When you call `greet("Mira")`, the value `"Mira"` flows into `name`, making the function flexible and reusable for any input.

steps.heading: Sending results back
steps.body: A function can hand a value back to whoever called it using `return`: `function double(n) { return n * 2; }`. The expression `double(5)` evaluates to `10`, which you can store in a variable, print, or pass into another function.

steps.question: Given `function add(a, b) { return a + b; }`, what does `add(3, 4)` produce?

steps.heading: A shorter syntax
steps.body: Arrow functions are a more compact way to write functions, especially short ones: `const double = (n) => n * 2;` does exactly what a longer `function` definition would, but with less typing. The `=>` ("arrow") replaces the `function` keyword and curly braces when the body is a single expression.

steps.heading: When you'll see them
steps.body: Arrow functions are everywhere in modern JavaScript — especially as quick, throwaway functions passed into other functions, like `numbers.map(n => n * 2)`. You don't have to use them, but you'll need to recognize them when reading real-world code.

steps.question: Which arrow function correctly returns the square of a number?

steps.heading: Asking true-or-false questions
steps.body: Comparison operators let your code ask questions about values and get back `true` or `false`: `5 > 3` is `true`, `5 === 3` is `false`. The `===` operator checks if two values are exactly equal — use it instead of `==`, which can produce confusing results by converting types behind the scenes.

steps.heading: Combining conditions
steps.body: Logical operators let you combine multiple true/false checks: `&&` (AND) is true only if both sides are true, `||` (OR) is true if at least one side is true, and `!` (NOT) flips a value. For example, `age >= 13 && age <= 19` checks whether someone is a teenager.

steps.question: What does `10 === "10"` evaluate to in JavaScript?
options: true, because they look the same
false, because === checks both value and type

steps.heading: Branching your code
steps.body: An `if` statement runs a block of code only when a condition is true: `if (score > 50) { console.log("Pass"); }`. Add an `else` to handle the opposite case: `else { console.log("Try again"); }`. Together, they let your program make decisions and react differently to different situations.

steps.heading: Chaining with else if
steps.body: When you have more than two possibilities, chain conditions with `else if`: `if (score >= 90) { grade = "A"; } else if (score >= 80) { grade = "B"; } else { grade = "C"; }`. JavaScript checks each condition top to bottom and runs the first block whose condition is true.

steps.question: Given `let score = 85;` and the chain from the lesson, what value does `grade` end up with?

steps.heading: Repeating with control
steps.body: A `for` loop repeats a block of code a set number of times: `for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) { console.log(i); }` prints `0` through `4`. The three parts inside the parentheses set a starting point (`let i = 0`), a condition to keep going (`i < 5`), and a step to take each time (`i++`, which adds 1).

steps.heading: Why loops matter
steps.body: Without loops, processing a list of 100 items would mean writing the same line 100 times. A loop lets you say "do this for every item" once, and JavaScript handles the repetition — making your code shorter, more flexible, and far less error-prone.

steps.question: How many times does `for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { console.log(i); }` run its body?

title: The while Loop and Loop Control

steps.heading: Looping on a condition
steps.body: A `while` loop keeps running as long as its condition stays true: `while (energy > 0) { energy = energy - 1; }`. Unlike a `for` loop, you don't set a fixed count up front — the loop checks the condition before each pass and stops the moment it becomes false.

steps.heading: Breaking out early
steps.body: Sometimes you need to exit a loop before its natural end. The `break` keyword stops the loop immediately, while `continue` skips to the next pass without finishing the current one. Forgetting to update the condition inside a `while` loop is a classic way to create an infinite loop that never stops.

steps.question: What's the danger of writing `while (true) { console.log("hi"); }` without a `break`?
options: It creates an infinite loop that never stops

steps.heading: A box for many values
steps.body: An array is an ordered list of values stored under one variable name: `let colors = ["red", "green", "blue"];`. Each item has a position, called an index, starting at `0` — so `colors[0]` is `"red"` and `colors[2]` is `"blue"`.

steps.heading: Reading and changing items
steps.body: You can read any item by its index, find out how many items there are with `.length`, and change an item by assigning to its position: `colors[1] = "yellow";` replaces `"green"` with `"yellow"`. Arrays are the go-to tool whenever you need to work with a collection of related values.

steps.question: Given `let colors = ["red", "green", "blue"];`, what does `colors[1]` equal?

steps.heading: Transforming with map
steps.body: Arrays come with built-in methods that save you from writing loops by hand. `.map()` creates a new array by applying a function to every item: `[1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2)` produces `[2, 4, 6]`. The original array stays unchanged — `.map()` always returns a brand-new one.

steps.heading: Filtering and searching
steps.body: `.filter()` builds a new array containing only the items that pass a test: `[1, 2, 3, 4].filter(n => n > 2)` produces `[3, 4]`. `.find()` returns the first matching item instead of a whole array. Together, `.map()` and `.filter()` cover most everyday array tasks without a single hand-written loop.

steps.question: What does `[1, 2, 3, 4].filter(n => n > 2)` produce?

steps.heading: Storing related details together
steps.body: An object groups related information into named properties: `let player = { name: "Mira", level: 5, active: true };`. Unlike an array's numeric positions, each value has a descriptive key — `player.name` is `"Mira"`, and `player.level` is `5`.

steps.heading: Reading and updating properties
steps.body: You can access a property with dot notation (`player.level`) or bracket notation (`player["level"]`), and update it the same way: `player.level = 6;`. Objects are how JavaScript represents real-world things — a user, a product, a game character — as a single, organized value.

steps.question: Given `let player = { name: "Mira", level: 5 };`, how do you access the player's level?

steps.heading: Setting the stage for tests
steps.body: A fixture is a reusable block that prepares a known environment before a test runs and cleans up afterward — logging in a user, opening a page, seeding data — so each test starts from a consistent state without repeating the same setup steps over and over.

steps.heading: Why not just repeat the setup code?
steps.body: Copy-pasting setup into every test file works at first, but the moment that setup needs to change — say, the login flow gains a new field — you'd have to update every single test that uses it. Fixtures keep that logic in one place, so a single change ripples out automatically.

steps.question: What is the main purpose of a fixture in testing?
options: To slow tests down for accuracy
To provide a consistent, reusable setup and teardown for tests
To replace the need for assertions

steps.heading: page, browser, and context
steps.body: Playwright Test ships with built-in fixtures like `page` (a single browser tab), `context` (an isolated browser session), and `browser` (the running browser instance). Most tests only need `page`: `test('loads homepage', async ({ page }) => { await page.goto('/'); });` — Playwright creates and tears it down for you automatically.

steps.heading: Why isolation matters
steps.body: Each test gets a fresh `context` and `page` by default, so cookies, local storage, and session state from one test never leak into another. That isolation is what lets a suite of hundreds of tests run without one test's leftover state quietly breaking the next.

steps.question: Why does each Playwright test get a fresh `page` and `context` by default?
options: So tests don't interfere with each other through shared state
Because browsers can only open one page at a time
To save memory on the test server

steps.heading: Extending the base test
The role of `use`
steps.body: The `use` function is how a fixture hands control to the test. Code written before `await use(page)` is the setup; code written after it (if any) is the teardown, run once the test finishes. Keeping setup and cleanup tied together in one block makes fixtures easy to read and trust.

steps.question: In a custom fixture, what does the code written AFTER `await use(page)` represent?
options: Code that runs before the test starts
Teardown code that runs after the test finishes

title: What is the Page Object Model?

steps.heading: Wrapping pages in objects
steps.body: The Page Object Model (POM) is a design pattern where each page (or component) of an app gets its own class that wraps its elements and actions. Instead of scattering raw selectors through every test, a test calls a readable method like `loginPage.login('demo', 'pass123')`.

steps.heading: Why it pays off
steps.body: When the UI changes — a button's selector, a form's layout — you update the page object once, and every test built on top of it keeps working. POM keeps tests focused on *what* they're checking, not *how* to find and click things.

steps.question: What is the main benefit of the Page Object Model?
options: It makes tests run in parallel automatically
It centralizes page interactions so UI changes only require one update
It removes the need for fixtures

steps.heading: Anatomy of a page object
Locators as properties
steps.body: Storing locators as properties (like `this.username`) means each selector is defined exactly once. If the username field's ID changes from `#username` to `#user-input`, you update one line inside the page object — not every test that touches the login form.

steps.question: In the LoginPage example, what does calling `loginPage.login('demo', 'pass123')` do?
options: Fills the username and password fields and clicks submit

steps.heading: Tests that read like user stories
Composing multiple page objects
steps.body: Larger flows often span several pages. A checkout test might lean on a `CartPage`, then a `CheckoutPage`, then an `OrderConfirmationPage` — each handling its own piece, while the test stitches the whole story together step by step.

steps.question: Why is `await loginPage.login('demo', 'pass123')` easier to read than raw selector calls in the test?
options: Because it hides what the test is actually checking
Because it expresses the user's intent without exposing selector details
Because it runs faster than raw selectors
Because it skips the assertion step

steps.heading: One test, many inputs
steps.body: Data-driven testing means running the same test logic against a list of different inputs, instead of writing a near-identical test for each case. To check that a login form rejects five invalid email formats, you don't write five tests — you write one test and feed it five values.

steps.heading: Less duplication, easier maintenance
steps.body: When the assertion logic changes, you update it in one place instead of five. And adding a new case is as simple as adding one more entry to the data list — no copy-pasting an entire test block just to check one more input.

steps.question: What is the core idea behind data-driven testing?
options: Running tests faster by skipping setup
Reusing one test's logic across multiple sets of input data
Testing only with real production data

title: Looping Over Test Data with forEach

steps.heading: Generating tests from an array
Readable failure reports
steps.body: Because each generated test gets its own name from the template string, a failure clearly tells you *which* input broke things — `rejects invalid email: missing@dot` — instead of leaving you to guess which of several checks inside one giant test went wrong.

steps.question: In the example above, how many separate tests does Playwright generate from the `invalidEmails` array?

steps.heading: Many tests, at the same time
steps.body: Instead of running test files one after another, Playwright can run many of them at once across multiple "workers" — separate processes that each handle a slice of the suite. A suite that takes 20 minutes serially might finish in 4 minutes spread across 5 workers.

steps.heading: Configuring workers
steps.body: You control parallelism through configuration, such as `workers: 4` in `playwright.config.js`, or a flag like `npx playwright test --workers=4`. More workers can mean faster runs, but each one consumes CPU and memory, so the right number depends on the machine or CI runner.

steps.question: What is a 'worker' in the context of running Playwright tests in parallel?
options: A person who writes the tests
A separate process that runs a portion of the test suite concurrently
A function that retries failed tests

steps.heading: Why shared state breaks parallel runs
steps.body: If two tests running at the same time both try to create a user named `"testuser"` in the same database, one will likely fail — not because the feature is broken, but because the tests collided. Parallel execution only works reliably when tests don't depend on shared, mutable state.

steps.heading: Designing for isolation
steps.body: Common fixes include generating unique data per test (like `testuser-${Date.now()}`), using separate accounts or databases per worker, and avoiding tests that depend on the order or side effects of other tests. A well-isolated test passes whether it runs alone or alongside a hundred others.

steps.question: Why might two tests fail when run in parallel, even though each passes fine on its own?
options: They may be colliding over shared, mutable state like a database record
Parallel tests always run in a different browser

steps.heading: Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery
steps.body: CI/CD is the practice of automatically building, testing, and often deploying code every time it changes — usually triggered by pushing to a shared repository. Continuous Integration (CI) means automatically running your test suite on every change; Continuous Delivery/Deployment (CD) means automatically preparing or shipping the verified result.

steps.heading: Why automated tests matter here
steps.body: A CI pipeline is only as trustworthy as the tests it runs. Automated Playwright tests give it a fast, repeatable way to answer "does this change break anything in the browser?" before a human ever looks — catching regressions within minutes of a push.

steps.question: In CI/CD, what does 'CI' (Continuous Integration) primarily refer to?
options: Automatically building and running tests every time code changes

steps.heading: Running headless and reproducibly
steps.body: CI servers don't have a visible screen, so tests run "headless" — the browser runs without a graphical window, which is also Playwright's default. A typical CI step installs dependencies, installs browsers with `npx playwright install --with-deps`, and then runs `npx playwright test`.

steps.heading: Settings that matter in CI
steps.body: CI configurations often differ from local ones — enabling retries for flaky network conditions (`retries: 2`), limiting workers to match the runner's CPU count, and forbidding `test.only` so a forgotten "focus on this test" line can't silently skip the rest of the suite (`forbidOnly: !!process.env.CI`).

steps.question: Why do Playwright tests typically run in 'headless' mode on a CI server?
options: Headless mode runs tests more slowly on purpose
CI servers have no graphical display, so the browser runs without a visible window
Headless mode is required to use page objects

steps.heading: What a test run leaves behind
steps.body: When a CI run finishes, Playwright can produce an HTML report summarizing every test's outcome, plus "artifacts" like screenshots, videos, and traces captured at the moment of failure. These let you see exactly what the browser looked like when something broke — without reproducing it locally.

steps.heading: Using traces to debug failures
steps.body: A Playwright trace is a step-by-step recording of a run — every action, network request, and DOM snapshot — that you can open in the Trace Viewer and scrub through like a video. For a test that fails only in CI and never locally, a trace is often the fastest way to see what really happened.

steps.question: Why are screenshots, videos, and traces especially useful for tests that fail only in CI?
options: They make the test suite run faster
They let you see exactly what happened during the failure without needing to reproduce it locally
They automatically fix the failing test
They replace the need for assertions

steps.heading: Programs talking to programs
steps.body: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined way for one piece of software to request services or data from another. It's like a restaurant menu — you don't need to know how the kitchen works, just what you can order and what you'll get back.

steps.heading: Web APIs and JSON
steps.body: Most modern web APIs communicate over HTTP and exchange data as JSON, a lightweight text format like `{"name": "Mira", "level": 5}` that's easy for both humans and machines to read. When a weather widget loads on a webpage, it's likely calling a weather API behind the scenes and rendering the JSON it gets back.

steps.question: What does an API let one piece of software do?
options: Request services or data from another piece of software through a defined interface
Replace the need for a database

steps.heading: Where requests go
steps.body: An API endpoint is a specific URL that represents a resource or action, like `https://api.example.com/users` (a list of users) or `https://api.example.com/users/42` (one specific user). The URL's structure tells you what you're asking for.

steps.heading: Base URLs and paths
steps.body: APIs usually share a common base URL, like `https://api.example.com`, with different paths branching off it — `/users`, `/orders`, `/products/7`. Reading an endpoint's path is often enough to guess what it returns, even before you make the request.

steps.question: Given the endpoint `https://api.example.com/orders/19`, what does it most likely represent?

steps.heading: Data as text
steps.body:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) represents data using objects (`{}`) and arrays (`[]`):

{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Mira",
  "skills": ["JavaScript", "Testing"]
}

This looks just like the JavaScript objects and arrays you already know — because JSON's syntax is based directly on them.

steps.heading: Parsing JSON in JavaScript
steps.body: When your code receives JSON as text, `JSON.parse()` converts it into a real JavaScript object you can work with: `let user = JSON.parse(responseText); console.log(user.name);` prints `"Mira"`. The reverse, `JSON.stringify()`, turns an object back into JSON text to send.

steps.question: Given `let data = JSON.parse('{"name": "Mira", "level": 5}');`, what does `data.level` equal?

steps.heading: Asking for data with GET
steps.body: GET requests ask a server to return data without changing anything — like visiting `https://api.example.com/users` to fetch a list of users. GET requests are "safe": making the same one twice should produce the same result and leave no side effects.

steps.heading: Creating data with POST
steps.body: POST requests send data to the server to create something new — like submitting a sign-up form to create a new account. Unlike GET, a POST typically changes server-side state, and sending the same POST twice might create two records instead of one.

steps.question: Which HTTP method would you use to fetch a list of users without changing anything?

steps.heading: Updating existing data
steps.body: PUT and PATCH both update existing resources, but differ in scope: PUT typically replaces an entire resource with new data, while PATCH updates only the fields you specify. If you only want to change a user's email, PATCH is the better fit — PUT might wipe out fields you didn't include.

steps.heading: Removing data
steps.body: DELETE requests ask the server to remove a resource, like `DELETE /users/42` to remove user 42. Like POST, DELETE changes server state — and most APIs require you to be authenticated before they'll honor one.

steps.question: If you want to update only a user's email address without affecting their other fields, which method is the better fit?
options: PUT, because it replaces the whole resource
PATCH, because it updates only the specified fields
DELETE, because it removes the resource

steps.heading: CRUD and HTTP methods
steps.body:
Many APIs map directly onto CRUD operations — Create, Read, Update, Delete:
Create maps to POST, Read maps to GET, Update maps to PUT or PATCH, and Delete maps to DELETE. Recognizing this pattern makes it much easier to predict what an unfamiliar API expects.

steps.heading: Methods carry meaning
steps.body: Choosing the right method isn't just a technicality — it tells the server, and anyone reading your code, what you intend to do. A GET that secretly deletes data would violate expectations baked into the web's infrastructure, breaking caching, retries, and tooling that all assume GET is safe.

steps.question: In the CRUD pattern, which HTTP method typically corresponds to 'Create'?

steps.heading: Five families of responses
steps.body: Every HTTP response includes a 3-digit status code that summarizes what happened, grouped into five families by their first digit: 1xx (informational), 2xx (success), 3xx (redirection), 4xx (client error — you made a mistake), and 5xx (server error — the server made a mistake). The family alone tells you a lot before you even read the details.

steps.heading: The codes you'll see most
steps.body: Day to day, you'll mostly meet `200 OK` (success), `201 Created` (a new resource was made), `400 Bad Request` (your request was malformed), `401 Unauthorized` (you need to authenticate), `404 Not Found` (the resource doesn't exist), and `500 Internal Server Error` (something broke server-side).

steps.question: A response comes back with status code 404. What does that tell you?
options: The request succeeded and created something
The requested resource could not be found
You need to wait and retry

steps.heading: Whose fault is it?
steps.body: The first digit of a status code tells you where to look when something goes wrong. A `4xx` code means the request itself was the problem — a typo in the URL, missing authentication, invalid data — so fixing it means changing what you send. A `5xx` code means the server failed while handling a valid request, so the fix is on the server's side.

steps.heading: Reading codes alongside messages
steps.body: Status codes are deliberately terse, so APIs usually pair them with a JSON body explaining what went wrong, like `{"error": "Email is required"}`. Combining the code's category with that message tells you both *who* needs to act and *what* needs to change.

steps.question: You send a request and get back `400 Bad Request`. Where should you look first to fix the problem?
options: Your own request — something about it was malformed or invalid

title: Sending Data in a Request Body

steps.heading: Beyond the URL
steps.body: GET requests typically carry all their information in the URL, but POST, PUT, and PATCH requests usually carry a request body — a chunk of data (often JSON) sent along with the request. Creating a new user might mean sending `{"name": "Mira", "email": "mira@example.com"}` as the body of a POST request.

steps.heading: Headers tell the server what to expect
steps.body: Alongside a body, requests include headers — metadata about the request. The `Content-Type: application/json` header tells the server "the body you're about to read is JSON," so it knows how to parse it. Forgetting this header is a common source of confusing "why isn't my data being saved?" bugs.

steps.question: What is the purpose of a request body in a POST request?
options: To specify which browser sent the request
To carry the data being sent to the server, such as a new resource's details
To redirect the user to another page

steps.heading: fetch and request options
Handling the response
steps.body:
`fetch()` returns a promise that resolves to a response object, which itself has a `.json()` method for parsing the body:

fetch('/users/42')
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(user => console.log(user.name));

This two-step "get the response, then parse its body" pattern shows up in nearly every fetch call you'll write.

steps.question: In the POST example above, why is `JSON.stringify()` used on the data before sending it?
options: Because fetch() requires the body to be a string, and stringify converts the object to JSON text
Because it changes GET to POST
Because it validates the email address

steps.heading: Proving who you are
steps.body: Authentication is how an API verifies who's making a request — telling a legitimate user apart from a stranger. Without it, anyone could read private data or make changes on someone else's behalf, simply by knowing an endpoint's URL.

steps.heading: Authentication vs. authorization
steps.body: Authentication answers "who are you?"; authorization answers "what are you allowed to do?" A logged-in user is authenticated, but might still be denied access to an admin-only endpoint — that's authorization deciding they lack permission, even though the system knows exactly who they are.

steps.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
options: They mean exactly the same thing
Authentication verifies who you are; authorization decides what you're allowed to do
Authentication is for APIs; authorization is for websites

steps.heading: Proving identity with a key
steps.body: An API key is a unique string that identifies whoever is making a request — often included in a header like `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz` or as a query parameter. The server checks the key against its records, treating an invalid or missing key as a reason to reject the request with `401 Unauthorized`.

steps.heading: Tokens and expiration
steps.body: Many modern APIs use tokens — temporary credentials issued after a successful login, often with an expiration time. When a token expires, the client must obtain a new one, often by logging in again or using a "refresh token" — limiting how long a stolen credential stays useful.

steps.question: Why might an API issue a token that expires after a set time, instead of one that lasts forever?
options: To make the API run faster
To limit how long a stolen or leaked credential could be used
Because tokens cannot be stored otherwise
To reduce the size of JSON responses

steps.heading: Attaching credentials to a request
What happens when credentials are missing or invalid
steps.body: If the token is missing, expired, or invalid, the server typically responds with `401 Unauthorized`. A well-built client checks for this status and responds gracefully — for example, redirecting the user to a login screen — rather than showing a confusing blank page.

steps.question: What status code would you expect if you send a request with an expired or missing authentication token?

steps.heading: More than a conversation
steps.body: A chatbot answers questions in a back-and-forth conversation, but an AI agent goes further — it can take actions in the world to accomplish a goal: searching the web, running code, sending an email, updating a file. The defining trait of an agent isn't how smart it sounds, but whether it can *do* things on your behalf.

steps.heading: The agent loop
steps.body: Most agents follow a loop: observe the current situation, decide what to do next, take an action, then observe the result and decide again. This "think, act, observe" cycle repeats until the goal is reached — much like how a person works through a multi-step task.

steps.question: What's the key difference between a simple chatbot and an AI agent?
options: Agents can take actions to accomplish a goal, not just respond in conversation

title: The Role of the Language Model

steps.heading: The reasoning engine
steps.body: At the center of most AI agents is a large language model (LLM) — the same kind of model that powers chatbots. In an agent, the LLM acts as the "brain": it reads the current situation, reasons about what should happen next, and decides which action to take.

steps.heading: Instructions and goals
steps.body: An agent is typically given a goal in plain language — "find the cheapest flight to Tokyo next month" — along with instructions about how it should behave. The LLM uses that goal to guide every decision in the loop, from picking the first action to recognizing when the job is done.

steps.question: In an AI agent, what role does the language model typically play?
options: It reasons about the situation and decides what action to take next
It directly controls the computer's hardware
It replaces the need for a goal

steps.heading: How much freedom is too much?
steps.body: Agents can range from tightly scripted — always following the same steps — to highly autonomous, deciding their own steps to reach a goal. More autonomy means more flexibility, but also more risk, since an agent might take an unexpected or undesired action while pursuing its goal.

steps.heading: Why guardrails matter
steps.body: Well-designed agents include guardrails: limits on what actions they're allowed to take, confirmation steps before risky actions like deleting data or spending money, and clear boundaries around their goal. Guardrails let you benefit from an agent's autonomy without losing control over what it actually does.

steps.question: Why might an AI agent need guardrails, like requiring confirmation before deleting data?
options: To make the agent slower on purpose
To limit the risk of the agent taking unexpected or undesired actions
Because agents cannot understand instructions otherwise
To reduce the size of its memory

steps.heading: Giving an agent hands
steps.body: On its own, a language model can only generate text — it can't browse the web, run code, or check today's weather. A tool is a function the agent can call to actually do something: search the web, query a database, run a calculation, or send a message. Tools are what let an agent act, not just talk.

steps.heading: Describing tools to the model
steps.body: To use a tool, the model needs to know it exists, what it does, and what input it expects — usually given as a short description and a list of parameters, like a function named `searchWeb` that takes a `query` string. The model decides *when* to call a tool and *what* to pass it, based on the goal it's pursuing.

steps.question: Why does an AI agent need tools to accomplish real-world tasks?
options: Tools make the agent's responses sound more polished
On its own, a language model can only generate text — tools let it actually take action
Tools are required to train the model
Tools replace the need for a goal

steps.heading: Choosing and calling a tool
steps.body: When an agent decides a tool is needed, it produces a structured "tool call" — naming the tool and supplying arguments, like calling `searchWeb` with `{ "query": "weather in Tokyo" }`. The system runs the tool, and the result — the actual search results — is handed back to the agent to continue reasoning with.

steps.heading: Looping with results
steps.body: A single task often involves several tool calls in sequence: search for flights, then check prices, then compare dates. After each result comes back, the agent re-evaluates the situation and decides on the next action — building toward the goal one informed step at a time.

steps.question: After an agent calls a tool, what typically happens next?
options: The tool's result is returned to the agent, which uses it to decide on the next step
The agent forgets the original goal

steps.heading: Narrow and well-described
steps.body: A good tool does one thing clearly — `getWeather(city)` is easier for an agent to use correctly than a do-everything `doStuff(input)`. Clear names, focused responsibilities, and well-described parameters all help the model pick the right tool and use it correctly on the first try.

steps.heading: Returning useful results
steps.body: A tool's output matters as much as its input. Returning a clean, structured result — like `{ temperature: 18, condition: "cloudy" }` rather than a wall of raw HTML — makes it far easier for the agent to extract what it needs and reason about what to do next.

steps.question: Why is `getWeather(city)` generally easier for an agent to use correctly than a do-everything `doStuff(input)`?
options: It runs faster on the server
Its narrow, clearly-named purpose makes it easier for the model to pick correctly and use well

steps.heading: Remembering within a task
steps.body: Short-term memory is what an agent keeps track of during a single task — the goal, the steps taken so far, and the results of recent tool calls. Without it, an agent might call the same tool twice or forget what it just learned halfway through a multi-step job.

steps.heading: Remembering across tasks
steps.body: Long-term memory persists beyond a single conversation or task — like remembering a user's preferences, past requests, or facts learned in earlier sessions. This is usually stored outside the model itself, in a database or file, and retrieved when relevant rather than kept in the model's head at all times.

steps.question: What is the main difference between an agent's short-term and long-term memory?
options: Short-term memory is for math, long-term memory is for words
Short-term memory tracks the current task; long-term memory persists across tasks and sessions
Long-term memory is faster than short-term memory

steps.heading: The context window
steps.body: A language model can only consider a limited amount of text at once — its "context window." Everything the agent needs to reason about — the goal, instructions, tool results, and conversation history — has to fit inside that space, which means older or less relevant information sometimes has to be trimmed or summarized.

steps.heading: Choosing what to keep
steps.body: Because space is limited, well-designed agents are selective: they might summarize a long tool result instead of keeping it in full, or store details in long-term memory and only pull them back in when needed. Treating memory as a limited resource — not an infinite notebook — is part of designing an agent that stays focused and effective.

steps.question: Why can't an AI agent simply keep every piece of information it has ever encountered in its active memory?
options: The model's context window has a limited size, so not everything can fit at once
Agents are not allowed to remember anything

steps.heading: From a big goal to small steps
steps.body: A goal like "plan a weekend trip to Lisbon" is too large to act on directly — so an agent benefits from breaking it into smaller steps: check flight prices, find a hotel, look up things to do, then summarize it all. This is called task decomposition, and it's a core part of planning.

steps.heading: Planning before acting
steps.body: Some agents plan their entire approach upfront, listing every step before taking the first action. Others plan one step at a time, deciding what to do next based on what's happened so far. Each approach trades off predictability (plan-first) against adaptability (plan-as-you-go).

steps.question: What is 'task decomposition' in the context of AI agents?
options: Breaking a large goal into smaller, more manageable steps
Forgetting old tasks to save memory

steps.heading: Plans meet reality
steps.body: Real-world tasks rarely go exactly as planned — a tool might return an error, a search might come back empty, or a step might reveal that the original plan needs to change. A good agent notices when something doesn't match expectations and adjusts, rather than blindly continuing down a broken path.

steps.heading: Retrying and replanning
steps.body: When something goes wrong, an agent might retry the same action in case it was a temporary glitch, try a different approach — search a different way, use a different tool — or revise its plan entirely based on what it just learned. Recognizing *which* response fits the situation is what separates a resilient agent from a brittle one.

steps.question: What should a well-designed agent do if a tool call returns an unexpected error?
options: Stop working entirely and report nothing
Recognize the problem and adapt — retry, try another approach, or revise the plan
Ignore the error and continue as if nothing happened
Delete the tool from its list permanently

steps.heading: One agent, many roles
steps.body: A single agent can be given a broad goal and left to figure out the steps — researching, drafting, and reviewing its own work in sequence. This keeps things simple, but can strain a single model trying to juggle very different kinds of tasks at once.

steps.heading: Splitting work across agents
steps.body: A multi-agent system assigns different roles to different agents — one researches, one writes, one reviews — each focused on a narrower job it can do well. They communicate by passing results to each other, similar to how a team of specialists might divide up a project.

steps.question: What is one potential benefit of splitting a complex task across multiple specialized agents instead of using one general agent?
options: It guarantees the task will be completed faster in every case
Each agent can focus on a narrower job suited to its role, rather than juggling everything at once
It removes the need for any tools

steps.heading: Mapping the steps
steps.body: Designing a workflow means deciding what stages a task passes through, which agent or tool handles each stage, and how information flows between them — for example: a research agent gathers facts, a writing agent drafts a summary, and a review agent checks it for accuracy before it's shown to the user.

steps.heading: Handoffs and checkpoints
steps.body: At each handoff, one agent's output becomes another's input — so the format and clarity of that output matters. Adding checkpoints, like a review step before anything reaches the user, helps catch mistakes early and keeps the whole workflow trustworthy, not just each piece individually.

steps.question: In a multi-agent workflow, why does the format and clarity of one agent's output matter?
options: It doesn't — agents can interpret anything
Because that output becomes the input to the next agent or step in the workflow
Because it determines how the agent is paid
Because only the final agent's output is ever used

steps.heading: Did it actually work?
steps.body: Building an agent is only half the job — you also need to check whether it reliably accomplishes its goal. This might mean running it against a set of test scenarios, checking whether its final answers are correct, or reviewing the steps it took to spot inefficiencies or mistakes.

steps.heading: Iterating based on what you find
steps.body: When an agent fails or behaves oddly, the fix might be a clearer goal description, a better-designed tool, an added guardrail, or a smarter memory strategy — rarely a single magic switch. Treating agent-building as an iterative cycle of testing and refining is what turns a rough first attempt into something genuinely useful.

steps.question: Why is testing an AI agent against a set of scenarios important after building it?
options: It makes the agent run faster
It helps reveal whether the agent reliably accomplishes its goal so it can be improved
It is required before an agent can use any tools
It replaces the need for a clear goal
